g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of Latches - the fundamental building blocks of memory in digital electronics.
A latch is a bistable circuit with two stable states - 0 or 1 - capable of storing one bit of data permanently until instructed to change.
Combinational circuits have no memory. Latches enable sequential circuits to remember past states, building counters, registers, and processors.
The SR Latch is the simplest latch type, built using two cross-coupled NOR gates. It has Set and Reset inputs to control the output.
g.text: SR Latch - The Simplest Memory Element

g.data-narration: The SR Latch truth table shows: Set forces Q=1, Reset forces Q=0, Hold maintains previous state, and invalid state occurs when both inputs are 1.
When S=R=1, both outputs become 0 - violating the complementary relationship. When inputs return to 0, the state becomes unpredictable.
The Gated SR Latch adds an Enable signal. When E=0, inputs are blocked and latch holds state. When E=1, inputs control the output.
g.text: Adds control over WHEN the latch responds to inputs

g.data-narration: The D Latch solves the invalid state problem by using a single data input D. The inverter ensures S and R are always complementary.
g.text: Eliminates invalid state by using a single Data input with automatic complement

g.data-narration: The D Latch is transparent when Enable=1 - the output follows the input. When Enable=0, it holds the last value of D.
Understanding the critical difference: Latches are level-sensitive, Flip-flops are edge-triggered - they sample inputs only at clock transitions.
Key takeaways: Latches store 1 bit using feedback, SR latch has forbidden state, D latch is most practical, and latches differ from flip-flops by being level-sensitive.
Real-world applications include switch debouncing, I/O registers, and they form the foundation for all computer memory and processor state machines.
In our next module, we'll explore Flip-Flops - the edge-triggered building blocks that build upon these latch concepts.
g.text: Next: Flip-Flops - Building on Latch Fundamentals →
